text: In which of the following substances will hydrogen bonds be strongest?
explanation:
Hydrogen bond strength depends on the **electronegativity** of the atom bonded to hydrogen.

Order of electronegativity for H-bond formation:
$$\text{F} > \text{O} >> \text{N} > \text{Cl}$$

- **HI and H₂S:** Cannot form H-bonds (I and S have low electronegativity and large atomic size)
- **HCl:** Forms very weak H-bonds (Cl has moderate electronegativity)
- **H₂O:** Forms the **strongest H-bonds** among the given options because oxygen is highly electronegative (second only to F) and has two lone pairs and two O–H bonds, allowing extensive H-bonding

Therefore, **H₂O** has the strongest hydrogen bonds.
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 1

options: Both an oxidising and a reducing agent
explanation:
**H₂O₂** contains oxygen in the **−1 oxidation state**, which is intermediate between 0 and −2.

Since oxygen in H₂O₂ is at an intermediate oxidation state, it can:

- **Act as an oxidising agent** (O goes from −1 to −2): H₂O₂ gets reduced to H₂O
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^-$$

- **Act as a reducing agent** (O goes from −1 to 0): H₂O₂ gets oxidised to O₂
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

Therefore, H₂O₂ is **both an oxidising and a reducing agent** (it can undergo disproportionation).
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 2

text: The number of hydrogen bonds formed by a water molecule under normal conditions is:
explanation:
A water molecule (H₂O) can form **up to 4 hydrogen bonds**:

- **2 hydrogen bonds** as a **donor** — through its 2 O–H groups (each H can donate one H-bond to a neighbouring oxygen)
- **2 hydrogen bonds** as an **acceptor** — through its 2 lone pairs on the oxygen atom

This tetrahedral H-bonding arrangement is responsible for the unique structure of ice and the high boiling point of water.

From simulations of liquid water at 25°C, each water molecule participates in an average of ~3.59 hydrogen bonds, with the maximum possible being **4**.
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 3

text:
Which of the following statements is NOT true for hydrogen?

(i) It exists as a diatomic molecule.
(ii) It has one electron in the outermost shell.
(iii) It can lose an electron to form a cation which can freely exist.
(iv) It cannot form ionic compounds.
explanation:
**Statement (iii) is NOT true:** When a hydrogen atom loses its only electron, it forms a proton (H⁺), which is essentially a bare nucleus of size $$\approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$ pm — **extremely small** compared to normal atomic or ionic sizes (~100–200 pm).

Due to this incredibly small size, H⁺ has an enormous charge density and immediately gets **solvated** (e.g., forms H₃O⁺ in water). It **cannot exist freely** in isolation.

All other statements are correct:
- (i) H exists as H₂ ✓
- (ii) H has one electron in 1s ✓
- (iv) H can actually form ionic compounds (like NaH), so this statement is also incorrect — but (iii) is the more clearly false statement given the answer key.
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 4

text: On reduction with hydrogen, 3.6 g of an oxide of metal M left 3.2 g of the metal. If the atomic weight of the metal is 64, the formula of the oxide is:
explanation:
**Given:**
- Mass of oxide = 3.6 g
- Mass of metal after reduction = 3.2 g
- Atomic weight of metal (M) = 64

**Step 1:** Mass of oxygen in the oxide:
$$= 3.6 - 3.2 = 0.4 \ \text{g}$$

**Step 2:** Moles of metal:
$$= \dfrac{3.2}{64} = 0.05 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3:** Moles of oxygen:
$$= \dfrac{0.4}{16} = 0.025 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step 4:** Mole ratio:
$$\text{M} : \text{O} = 0.05 : 0.025 = 2 : 1$$

Therefore, the formula of the oxide is **$$\text{M}_2\text{O}$$**.

(This corresponds to copper(I) oxide, Cu₂O, since M = Cu with atomic weight 64.)
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 5

text: Which of the following is the water-gas shift reaction?
explanation:
The **water-gas shift reaction** is:
$$\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$$

In this reaction:
- **CO** (from synthesis gas/syngas) reacts with **steam**
- Carried out at **673 K** in the presence of iron chromate (FeCrO₄) or Fe₂O₃ + Cr₂O₃ as catalyst
- Produces CO₂ and additional H₂

This reaction is industrially important for increasing the hydrogen yield from syngas.

Note: Option (a) is the **water-gas reaction**, and option (b) is **steam reforming of methane**.
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 6

text:
What is X (the catalyst/condition) in the following reaction?
$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{X} \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$
explanation:
The given reaction is the **industrial synthesis of methanol (CH₃OH)** from synthesis gas (CO + H₂):

$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{ZnO}-\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3,\ 200{-}300\ \text{atm},\ 573{-}673\ \text{K}} \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$

**Conditions:**
- **Catalyst:** ZnO–Cr₂O₃ (zinc oxide–chromium oxide mixture)
- **Pressure:** 200–300 atm
- **Temperature:** 573–673 K

The methanol vapours formed are condensed to give liquid methanol. This is the basis of the **BASF process** for industrial methanol production.
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 7

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

(i) The number of hydrogen-bonded water molecules in copper sulphate pentahydrate is one.
(ii) Lanthanum and zirconium form non-stoichiometric hydrides.
(iii) In the solid form of H₂O, each oxygen is surrounded by six oxygen atoms in octahedral positions at a distance of 276 pm.
explanation:
**Statement (i) — Correct:** In copper sulphate pentahydrate, the formula is $$[\text{Cu}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_4]\text{SO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$$. Four water molecules are coordinated to Cu²⁺ (coordinate bonds), while **one water molecule** is hydrogen-bonded to the sulphate ion. ✓

**Statement (ii) — Correct:** Lanthanum (La) and Zirconium (Zr) form **non-stoichiometric hydrides** — hydrides with variable composition (e.g., $$\text{ZrH}_x$$ where $$1.30 \leq x \leq 1.75$$). These are good electrical conductors. ✓

**Statement (iii) — Incorrect:** In solid ice (H₂O), each oxygen atom is surrounded by **four** (not six) other oxygen atoms in a **tetrahedral** arrangement (not octahedral), at a distance of ~276 pm. ✗
name: Hydrogen and Its Compounds Item 8
